title: Lavender and honey scones
subtitle: Lightly floral scones sweetened with honey and fragrant with culinary lavender
excerpt: Dried culinary lavender gives these scones a gentle floral note without tipping into soap. Honey replaces part of the sugar, adding sweetness and a light stickiness to the crumb. The method is the standard rubbing-in technique for scones, keeping the butter cold throughout. They bake in 12 minutes from a cold oven. The whole process takes under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Lavender in sweet baking has Provencal roots, where the plant grows widely and has been used in confectionery and biscuits since the seventeenth century. Its use in British baking became widespread in the 1990s and 2000s, when honey-and-lavender flavour combinations appeared across artisan bakeries and food writing. The combination appears in Rachel Allen's 'Bake' (2008) and in Marguerite Patten's later work on flower-flavoured baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones for up to 1 month. Reheat from frozen at 180 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The cut rounds can be refrigerated overnight on a tray and baked the following morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried flowers from culinary varieties of lavender (usually Lavandula angustifolia), sold specifically for use in food. The quantity that gives a pleasant floral flavour is very small: about 1 teaspoon per 250 g flour. Beyond this the flavour quickly becomes soapy or medicinal. Use dried culinary lavender rather than decorative dried lavender from a florist, which may be treated with chemicals and is far more concentrated in aromatic compounds. Fresh lavender flowers can be used at double the quantity of dried.

content.text: is restraint. One teaspoon of dried flowers is enough for this batch; more and the scones will taste of soap. Rub the lavender into the sugar before adding the flour, which helps distribute the flowers evenly and bruises them slightly to release more fragrance.

Keep the butter cold throughout: cold butter in a hot oven creates steam pockets that lift the scone. If the butter softens while you are mixing, put the dough in the fridge for 10 minutes before cutting and baking.

Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan and place a baking tray inside to heat. Rub the 1 tsp lavender into the 25 g caster sugar between your fingers. Combine the 250 g flour, 1 tsp baking powder and pinch of salt. Rub in the 50 g cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the lavender sugar. Beat together the 1 egg, 30 g honey and 100 ml cold milk. Pour into the flour mixture and mix quickly with a fork until a soft dough forms. Do not overwork.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface and pat out to 2.5 cm thick without rolling. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the hot baking tray. Brush the tops with a little milk. Bake for 11 to 14 minutes until risen, deep golden brown on top, and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for 5 minutes before serving.

items.solution: Too much lavender was used, or the lavender was from a decorative rather than culinary source. Culinary lavender is milder and sold specifically for food use. Reduce to 0.5 tsp on the next batch and make sure the flowers are labelled as culinary.

The butter was soft rather than cold, or the dough was overworked. Cold butter creates steam pockets in the oven; soft butter blends into the dough and cannot create the same lift. If the kitchen is warm, refrigerate the cut rounds for 10 minutes before baking.

items.problem: Scones spread flat rather than rising
items.solution: The dough was too wet or the baking tray was cold. The tray should be preheated in the oven; the blast of heat on the base sets the scone before it can spread.
